title: AI Liquid Cooling Mandate — Vertiv and Modine Sold Out as Air Cooling Hits Physics Wall
summary: NVIDIA's 1,400W GB300 and 1,800W Rubin GPUs make direct-to-chip liquid cooling non-optional, and the two dominant suppliers, Vertiv and Modine, both posted record Q1 2026 backlogs ($15B and $4B respectively) with multi-year sold-out positions.
catalyst: Two events crystallized the thesis this week. On May 26, 2026, Modine reported Q4 FY2026 data center sales up 158% YoY and disclosed a $4 billion long-term chiller agreement with a major hyperscaler running through 2029 [1]. Two days later, Dell announced $24.4B in AI server orders for Q1 FY2027 and a $51.3B AI backlog with management explicitly calling out memory and components as the binding supply constraint [2] — implying that as soon as those constraints ease, the bottleneck moves to power and thermal infrastructure, which is sold out at Vertiv ($15B backlog, 2.9x book-to-bill) through 2027 [3].
thesis: AI rack-level power has crossed a physics threshold that ends the air-cooling era: GB300 NVL72 racks pull 132-140 kW with 1,400W GPUs, Rubin (H2 2026) reaches 1,800W per GPU, and NVIDIA's own product page now describes the GB300 NVL72 as a 'fully liquid-cooled, rack-scale architecture' [4]. Direct-to-chip liquid cooling kits for a single GB300 NVL72 rack cost roughly $50,000 [5], creating a multi-billion-dollar TAM that Vertiv and Modine have effectively pre-sold via their backlogs. Both companies just raised FY guidance materially — Vertiv to $13.5-14.0B revenue with 51% earnings growth [3], Modine to FY27 adjusted EBITDA of $650-680M (38-44% growth) with data center sales rising 60-80% [1] — and neither valuation fully discounts a multi-year sold-out position.

ticker_rationale.VRT: Vertiv's Q1 2026 backlog more than doubled YoY to $15 billion at a 2.9x book-to-bill ratio, with adjusted operating margin expanding 430 bps to 20.8% and Americas data center revenue up 44% [3]. The April 27, 2026 acquisition of Strategic Thermal Labs — a specialist in cold-plate design and server-side liquid cooling — plus ThermoKey for heat rejection, plugs Vertiv directly into the chip-level thermal stack [6]. At 31.5x forward P/E with 41.8% ROE and a screener score of 82 (BUY), VRT trades at a reasonable multiple given the sold-out order book and 51% guided earnings growth in 2026.
ticker_rationale.MOD: Modine just signed a $4 billion long-term chiller agreement with a major hyperscale customer running through 2029 — disclosed alongside Q4 FY26 results on May 26, 2026, in which data centers grew 158% YoY and Climate Solutions segment revenue jumped 87% to $665.9M [1]. FY27 guidance of 20-35% revenue growth and $650-680M adjusted EBITDA implies 38-44% EBITDA growth, with data center sales projected up 60-80%. At 33x forward P/E and a peg ratio of 1.03 (per the screener), MOD is reasonably valued for a company transforming from auto-parts thermal management to high-density AI cooling.

body_md:
## Setup

The AI infrastructure investment narrative has fixated on two bottlenecks — GPUs and power generation — while quietly skipping over a third that is now binding: the physical heat rejection from the rack. NVIDIA's current and forward roadmap has crossed a thermal threshold that air cooling cannot serve. The GB300 NVL72, NVIDIA's current flagship rack-scale AI platform, packs 72 Blackwell Ultra GPUs at 1,400W each and pulls 132-140 kW per rack under normal operation [4][5]. NVIDIA's own GB300 NVL72 product page now describes the system as a 'fully liquid-cooled, rack-scale architecture' [4] — language that did not appear for prior-generation Hopper-class systems. The Vera Rubin architecture, expected to reach cloud providers in H2 2026, pushes that further to roughly 1,800W per GPU, making direct-to-chip liquid cooling non-negotiable [5].

This is not a marginal upgrade cycle. It is a forced architectural shift, and the two pure-play beneficiaries — Vertiv and Modine — both delivered Q1 2026 results that show backlogs growing far faster than industry shipments, with multi-year sold-out positions and meaningfully raised guidance.

## Why Now

Three pieces of evidence converged in the last 30 days that move this from a structural story to a near-term catalyst.

First, Modine on May 26, 2026 reported Q4 FY2026 net sales of $954.4 million (up 47% YoY) and adjusted EPS of $1.71 (up 53%). Climate Solutions segment revenue jumped 87% to $665.9 million, with the data center sub-segment up 158% YoY [1]. Critically, Modine disclosed a $4 billion long-term chiller agreement with a major hyperscale customer covering calendar 2027 through 2029 [1] — a multi-year commitment that locks in revenue visibility well beyond a normal industrial order book. FY2027 guidance was set at 20-35% revenue growth with adjusted EBITDA of $650-680M (38-44% growth), with data center sales expected to rise another 60-80% [1].

Second, Vertiv's Q1 2026 results (April 22, 2026) reported revenue of $2.65 billion (+30%), with project backlog more than doubling YoY to over $15 billion at a book-to-bill ratio of approximately 2.9x — meaning for every dollar of equipment shipped, nearly three dollars in new orders are landing [3]. Adjusted operating margin expanded 430 bps to 20.8%. Vertiv raised 2026 guidance to $13.5-14.0B in revenue, implying ~30% organic growth and 51% adjusted EPS growth [3]. Five days later, on April 27, 2026, Vertiv announced the acquisition of Strategic Thermal Labs (STL), a specialist in cold-plate design, server-side liquid cooling, and high-density thermal validation. Vertiv's Chief Product and Technology Officer Scott Armul stated explicitly: 'As AI and high-performance computing push power densities to unprecedented levels, understanding and solving heat challenges at the chip level becomes critical' [6]. A second acquisition of ThermoKey (Italian heat-exchanger specialist) followed, completing a vertical integration from the cold plate at the GPU die to the dry coolers on the data center roof.

Third, Dell Technologies on May 28, 2026 reported Q1 FY2027 results showing $24.4 billion in AI server orders for the quarter, $16.1 billion of AI server revenue recognized, and an ending AI backlog of $51.3 billion [2]. Management raised FY27 guidance to $165-169B in revenue and explicitly called out memory and other components as the primary supply constraint [2]. This is the demand signal: as upstream constraints loosen, the bottleneck moves downstream to power and thermal infrastructure, which is exactly where Vertiv and Modine already sit with sold-out books.

## Company-Specific Edge

**Vertiv (VRT)** is the dominant pure-play infrastructure provider, with capabilities spanning power conversion (UPS, switchgear, busways), thermal management (CDUs, liquid cooling, chillers), services, and now — post-STL and ThermoKey — chip-level cold plates and heat exchangers. The full-stack capability is structurally advantaged because hyperscalers increasingly procure complete rack-ready solutions rather than assembling best-of-breed components. The screener flags VRT with an Innovation score of 9/10, Infrastructure score of 9/10, and AI Resilience of 10/10 — the highest possible category in the IOWN framework. Free cash flow conversion is strong (199.5% EPS growth YoY per screener) and the 2026 margin expansion plan to 22-23% adjusted operating margin should drop billions in incremental cash flow.

**Modine (MOD)** has executed one of the most underappreciated strategic pivots in the industrial sector — moving from a legacy auto-parts thermal supplier (Performance Technologies) to a data center cooling powerhouse via the Climate Solutions segment. Data center revenue has gone from 4% to ~25% of total revenue between FY2021 and FY2025 and is now on track to push well past 30% in FY2027. The $4B hyperscaler agreement is the most consequential single contract in the company's history and provides 3+ years of revenue visibility in the highest-margin segment. The PEG ratio of 1.03 from the screener is striking — a true growth story trading at GARP-style valuation.

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | VRT | MOD |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 / Q4 FY26 revenue growth | +30% YoY | +47% YoY |
| Backlog / multi-year commitments | $15B backlog, 2.9x book-to-bill | $4B chiller agreement (2027-2029) |
| Data center sub-segment growth | Americas DC revenue +44% | +158% YoY |
| FY26 / FY27 revenue guide | $13.5-14.0B (+30% organic) | +20-35% growth |
| FY26 / FY27 earnings guide | EPS $6.30-6.40 (+51%) | Adj EBITDA $650-680M (+38-44%) |
| Forward P/E (screener) | 31.5x | 33.2x |
| ROE (screener) | 41.8% | 10.0% |
| IOWN overall score | 82 (BUY) | 77 (HOLD) |

## Bear Case

Three primary risks deserve attention.

First, competitive entry: liquid cooling looks lucrative, and competition is coming. Schneider Electric, Eaton, Trane, Johnson Controls and several Chinese vendors are all investing in liquid cooling capability. The current backlog reflects 2026-2028 shipments — beyond that, share will be contested. Vertiv's STL acquisition is a defensive move precisely because the company sees this risk.

Second, hyperscaler concentration: both companies depend heavily on a small number of hyperscaler customers. Modine's $4B agreement, while attractive, comes with the inverse risk of a single counterparty walking. Hyperscalers also have a history of squeezing supplier margins as volumes scale — pricing power may compress in years 2-3.

Third, valuation and execution risk: VRT at 31x forward P/E and MOD at 33x forward P/E both price in continued execution. Any slippage in the GB300/Rubin ramp at NVIDIA, or a slower hyperscaler capex cycle in 2027 if AI ROI proves disappointing, would compress multiples sharply. MOD's 3.4% net margin (per the screener) is thin and leaves limited buffer for cost overruns on rapid capacity expansion.

There is also a structural risk that AI inference-time efficiency gains (smaller models, distillation, more efficient transformer architectures) reduce GPU demand faster than the rack-scale infrastructure can be deployed. That would soften the back end of these backlogs.

## Conclusion

The AI infrastructure trade is broadening from chips and grid to the literal plumbing inside the rack. Vertiv and Modine are not speculative beneficiaries — they have already booked the revenue, signed the long-term agreements, and integrated vertically to capture the chip-level thermal interface. The thesis becomes harder, not easier, to underwrite the longer you wait: backlogs at Vertiv covered 12-18 months of revenue at the end of Q1, and Modine's $4B agreement runs through 2029. Buy this when the news flow is operationally confirming (it is, as of this week) and the multiples remain reasonable relative to the visibility you are getting (31-33x forward P/E for sold-out 30%+ organic growth businesses). Monitor for hyperscaler capex revisions, NVIDIA roadmap slippage, and Eaton/Schneider competitive announcements as the key signals that the thesis is weakening.
risks: Schneider Electric, Eaton, or Chinese vendors announce competitive liquid cooling product lines that pressure Vertiv/Modine pricing power on 2027+ orders
A single hyperscaler customer cancels or restructures a multi-year cooling agreement (Modine has explicit concentration risk on the $4B chiller deal)
AI inference efficiency gains (model distillation, smaller architectures) reduce GPU rack demand faster than infrastructure can be deployed, softening 2027 backlog conversion
NVIDIA Rubin roadmap slips meaningfully, delaying the H2 2026 ramp that drives the next leg of liquid cooling demand
Modine's thin 3.4% net margin leaves limited buffer if capacity expansion costs overrun or labor inflation pressures the data center cooling buildout
invalidation: Vertiv Q2 2026 backlog (reported late July 2026) declines sequentially below $14B or book-to-bill falls below 1.5x for two consecutive quarters
Modine cancels or materially restructures the $4B hyperscaler chiller agreement, or FY2027 data center segment growth comes in below 30% YoY for two consecutive quarters
NVIDIA announces a return to air-cooled rack architectures for the next Rubin-class platform, or specs out a GPU TDP below 1,200W (signaling thermal moderation)
A major hyperscaler (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) cuts 2027 AI capex guidance by 20%+ in any single quarterly update
